desc:
Here the road bends from the north to the east and continues straight east,
leaving the hill ridge to the north. To the south of the road you can see three
stones, obviously set there as marking signs. The stones are so weathered that
you can no longer read what was written on them once, but even if you could read
it, you would probably find it no longer true anyway.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The highest point of the hill ridge is just above you, but the slope leading to
it is an almost vertical rock wall which there seems to be no way to climb. To
the south of the road at some distance you can see a stone arch rising in the
middle of a grassy plain.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Following the bends of the feet of the hills, the Old East Road runs east-west
on its way from the Last Bridge to the River Bruinen. South of the road the
grassland stretches to the very horizon, patches of grey and patches of green
blending at a distance into one carpet of a grey-green colour.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The Old East Road is running east-west here. A hill with a wide flat crown rises
to the north of the road. On the other side of the road everything is overgrown
by weeds, but just to the south of you the thicket is cut in two by a wide
ribbon of land covered with a carpet of a heather-like plant.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road runs east-west here. A ridge of hills rises above you to the north.
Wide stone shelves lead up the side of the ridge allowing one to climb all the
way up to the top. Wormwood grows to the south of the road, swaying in the wind
and filling the air with a bitter smell.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Leaving to the north the last hill of the ridge, the Old East Road bends from
the west to the south, continuing its way through the grassy plains. On the
southern side of the road everything is overgrown with weeds; a heathery plain
stretches out to the east.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The Old East Road turns north-east here, winding along the foot of the hills.
Ages ago the road already ran through this land, and ages from now it will
probably still run through it, even when all the old walls and towers crumble
down, and no other trace of Men is left in the Lone-lands. Buildings wear down
from use, but a road gets stronger from use. Buildings suffer from their owners,
but a road cares not who uses it or for what purpose, for rain soon washes away
any traces and any defile. Buildings can be burned down or knocked down, but a
road can only be destroyed by a flood or an earthquake, and if destroyed, it
will probably be restored, while somewhere there are people who need to pass
through this land.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Heather grows on the side of the road, its faint fragrance unusually distinct
and sweet in the fresh air. A few heather plants have white flowers and look
slightly larger than the ordinary heather; these plants also seem to smell
stronger.
name: Heathery Slope

desc:
The road makes its way through the lands that are now called Lone-lands. They
have not always been called so; Men once dwelt here, ages ago. Legends tell that
they became an evil people, because they fell under the shadow of Angmar. But
all were destroyed in the war that brought the North Kingdom to its end. It is
said that the hills have forgotten them, but a shadow still lies on the land.
name: Old East Road

desc:
A strange kind of weeds dominates on this patch of grassland. Most of the plants
are from two to three feet high with very thick and tough stems and meaty
leaves, grey-green on the outside and silvery-grey on the inside. The flowers
are white five-petalled stars, disproportionately small for such tall stems and
thick leaves; something in their appearance gives them a mournful look.
name: Overgrown Grassland

desc:
Judging by the ditches that cut the ground at regular intervals, this must have
once been a field, cultivated to grow crops. Weeds have now taken this spot of
land for their own, claiming the place that once belonged to more civilised
plants. Some of these weeds are probably descendants of the plants that were
grown here by people: there is one that looks distinctly like corn but has
thorns on its stem and is of an unpleasant brownish hue.
name: Overgrown Grassland

desc:
This village had probably been destroyed in a war and abandoned ever since. No
building has survived the centuries of desolation, and you can only see where
the houses and streets had been by noticing where the ground rises or falls
slightly, or where the vegetation changes in type or in colour. One can only
wonder whether a great city fallen and abandoned would leave more traces in the
landscape, and whether its ruins would take much longer to overgrow than the
ruins of a small village.
name: Ruins of a Village

desc:
This had been a village once, but it was so long ago that all one can see is
where the buildings had been. This uneven square, slightly rising over the
ground and completely covered with weeds, must have been one building; that
rectangle must have been the other, and this uneven shape might have been a
cross-like base of a temple. This patch might have once been a main road or a
square because it continues the overgrown road that entered the village to the
north, and this one might have been a major street because it is covered by a
carpet of the same heather-like plant as the main road.
name: Ruins of a Village

desc:
Weeds of all sizes, shapes and colours grow here. To the west of you an ancient
cemetery lies behind a low wall. The wall is not more than two feet high and
seems to be purely decorative, yet it seems to have done its part in keeping the
weeds out of the cemetery and not letting it overgrow completely.
name: East of a Cemetery

desc:
Hundreds and hundreds of tombstones rise from the ground at this abandoned
cemetery, protected only by a low wall and open to the sky and to the wind. All
the tombstones here are blank, marked by no inscription or symbol. Whatever has
left these graves unnamed - necessity, accident or design - there is nothing
here to tell you what had happened, when or why.
name: Forgotten Cemetery

desc:
The weeds are not so thick here, and in places there are even patches of
ordinary green grass. To the east you can see a cemetery behind a low wall. The
wall is about one and a half feet high, obviously built to mark the borders of
the cemetery, but not to keep anyone out of it. The stones of the wall are
covered with moss and climbing flowers, little white and lilac bells with black
edges like black ribbons worn in mourning.
name: Grassland by a Cemetery Wall

desc:
The grassland rises slightly to the west, ascending the slope of a low hill.
Something seems to have stopped the ubiquitous weeds half-way through the
ascent, and only the plain green grass and a couple of field flowers made their
way to the top, where a few white birches proudly rise, crowning the level
slope.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
Tall white birches rise all around, an unexpected bright patch in the sombre
landscape where all hills and forests are dark or black, most grasses are grey,
and the sky seems to be bleak even on the sunny days. Somebody might have
planted these birches here for some reason, or else they might be one of the few
things still remaining from those long forgotten days when these lands had a
brighter appearance.
name: Clump of Birches

desc:
A patch of white attracts the gaze to the east, where a clump of birches rises
on the top of a low hill. By contrast, the forest to your north seems as dark as
if somebody had painted its trees black. Nature seems to have been reluctant to
allow such a sharp change of colour from black to white, and so it painted this
patch of land between the two patches of forest grey with a few occasional spots
of green.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
To the east and south the forest gradually thins and finally succumbs to
grass-covered plains. In the other directions the forest is still dark, dense
and brooding. Tracks of small animals can be seen here and there on the ground,
but it is hard to discern what type of animals made them.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Baleful primal trees of unholy size, age, and grotesqueness leer above you like
pillars of a temple. This is the edge of the forest and the trees have thinned.
Grey fields take over to the west and to the far north a large river flows down
from the Ettendales.
name: Edge of a Dark Forest

desc:
Bleak and grey plains stretch all the way to the southern horizon. The land here
looks rocky and vegetation is sparse. A dark forest with large twisted trees
grows to the east; at the border it looks thin, but further in it becomes almost
impenetrable. To the north-west lies the remains of old wheat fields, but no one
seems to have cultivated them for many years and now most of the top soil has
blown away.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
This is the southeastern corner of what once was a large cultivated area. To the
east of here lies a large and dense forest, while to the south a rocky and bleak
landscape takes over. However, to the far northwest distant mountains can be
seen on a clear day. A low pile of rocks, which was once a fence marking the
edge of a wheat field, hinders movement to the south.
name: Edge of the Fields

desc:
The huge trees that inhabit this dark and brooding forest stand all about you,
though they become more densely packed towards the east. While it is possible to
locate a grey field beyond the forest to the west, the branches make it hard to
distinguish many details. Several roots near the surface of the soft soil makes
walking across the forest somewhat hazardous.
name: Edge of a Dark Forest

desc:
This is the centre of a dark and brooding forest that bears an aspect more than
unusually sinister. Ancient lightning-scarred trees seem unnaturally large and
twisted, while the other vegetation is unnaturally thick and feverish. Of wild
creatures there are none.
name: Centre of a Dark Forest

desc:
Lightning seems to have struck the black trees of the forest here and there,
falling this tree, scarring that one, burning a spot on the ground here,
splitting a tree-stump there. The trees here are older than those to the north,
and some of them seem to be dry, but as their black leaves do not change colour
and do not fall off when they dry, one can barely tell the dead trees from the
living ones.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The forest ends sharply to the east, the few remaining trees scattered as if
retreating in haste. Just a few steps to the south of the last black tree some
white birches stand with such a proud look, as if they were an army that was set
there to beat back the unfriendly black forest and proved victorious in the
task.
name: Edge of the Dark Forest

desc:
The burial mound on the forest border is covered with green grass. Upon its
western side the grass is white as with a drifted snow: small flowers spring
here like countless stars amid the turf. Whoever rests in this green mound must
rest in peace now, for time treats the dead better than the living, and the
houses of the dead better than the houses of the living. Time erases towers,
temples, cities and villages, yet keeps intact a mound, a grave, a crypt, a
cemetery.
name: Grassy Mound

desc:
Rocks have been set in the ground at regular intervals, indicating that this
completely overgrown patch of land had once been of use to people. Centuries ago
the last people abandoned this land, letting it rest, and centuries might pass
till they return here and till somebody again turns this patch of grassland into
a field or a pasture.
name: Overgrown Grassland

desc:
A tall arch, made of plain grey stone, rises here, marking the entrance to the
cemetery that lies behind a low mossy wall. No inscription marks the arch, but a
strange sign is engraved on the top: a square with chipped corners, divided into
four parts. A few climbing plants have set root by the base of the arch and made
their way up the arch; their bell-like flowers are white or lilac with a black
border.
name: Before the Stone Arch

desc:
The ground is very even here, as if this was once a cultivated field. The soil
is moist, dark in colour and probably quite fertile, but nothing except weeds
grows here now. The weeds grow very thickly, making it hard to move. At some
distance to the north you can see the Old East Road running at the foot of a
ridge of hills, their dark heads sharply outlined across the sky.
name: Overgrown Grassland

desc:
Once this must have been a fairly wide and well-used road. But the only way to
notice that it was here before at all is by looking at the vegetation on the
ground. Around the road the land is completely overgrown by thick and thorny
weeds, whereas the road itself is covered by a carpet of some plants, which
would seem to be heather if most of their flowers were not white.
name: Overgrown Road

desc:
A silvery-grey, bitter-smelling sea of wormwood sways around you, a new wave
passing through it with every gust of the wind. To the northeast the Old East
Road passes through the terrain. To the west and the south the grassland changes
colour, as the wormwood blends with the other weeds.
name: Overgrown Grassland

desc:
As the trees to the north grow thinner, the stream becomes quicker and noisier,
as if it was eager to get out of the cheerless forest. For a while the stream
follows the curves of the forest ground, then rushes down from a steep bank,
slows down to a trickle on a level slope and so reaches the border of the forest
and the Old East Road.
name: A Small Forest Stream

desc:
Stumbling at the roots of the trees, fallen branches and rocks in the forest
soil, a small stream makes its way through the dark forest. The trees around the
stream are so still as if they were listening to the sound of flowing water.
name: A Stream in the Dark Forest

desc:
No oaks, beeches, or other cheerful green trees of sunlit lands grow in this
forest. Unfamiliar dark trees reach to the sky, their bark almost black, and
their black branches and foliage covering the sky in such an intricate pattern,
as if somebody cut it out of black paper or wrought out of black metal and then
glued to the sky or nailed to the tree-tops.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
While the dark forest has begun to thin it is still very dense, dark and
difficult to traverse. A small clearing has been created here when a tall lonely
tree was destroyed by lightning. The bones and chewed corpses of several small
mammals lie partly buried in the weedy, fulgurite-pitted earth, so apparently
some kind of predator roams these woods.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
These are the outskirts of ancient farmlands. These lands were most likely
abandoned when war resulted in the removal of the North Kingdom. The soil here
was once very fertile but now most of it has blown away leaving a thin layer
that contains a grey and unhealthy look. The old farmlands spread out for many
miles towards the south-west and finally ends at the edge of a narrow valley
that is bounded by the flow of the river Mitheithel.
name: Old Farmland

desc:
This is the centre of what was once a farm's huge field capable of growing
enough grain to feed a large population. That population disappeared when the
kingdom of Rhudaur was first absorbed by its larger neighbours and later
destroyed by them. Some strands of wheat still grow around here, but mostly the
vegetation consists of various types of weeds.
name: Old Farmland

desc:
To the south the remains of a rather large building that once stood two stories
high can be found. An old sign in a rusty doorplate is still legible. Around the
ruins, ancient oak trees that are several feet in diameter still grow, though
one of them has been uprooted and is leaning against the remainder of a three
feet tall wall. West from here is a smaller building that once served as a
stable.
name: Path to the Inn

desc:
This was once the Last Inn. Now all that remains are the stone walls, a roof and
a pile of rubble. In the centre of the old common room someone has cleared the
area by removing the debris and has then used some of this waste to form a crude
circular fireplace. Other than the remains of a recent fire, there are no signs
of recent habitation at the inn.
name: The Inn at the Last Bridge

desc:
This building used to be a smithy, but a fire raged here some time ago and since
then the building has remained a hollowed ruin for the stones in the walls
crumbled and cracked under the intense heat of the fire. On the blackened floor
there are some small puddles of metal left over from the days when this building
was used by the blacksmith. A door which opens in the western wall leads out
towards the small path.
name: Decaying Smithy

desc:
The canyon floor slopes heavily downwards to the north, and upwards to the east
-- towards the rocky hills. North of here the canyon seems to bend to the east,
far away. The canyon floor is covered by rubble and earth, and some tiny and
twisted plants grow here.
name: Narrow Canyon

desc:
Tall and steep these hills rise high above the Old East Road to the south. East
of here a very deep and narrow canyon splits the hills apart, and it seems to be
impossible to get to the bottom without risking life and limb. To the west
however, quiet plains slope from the north down towards the road. North of here
patches of trees form a forest, which grows thicker in that direction.
name: Steep Hills

desc:
The road is still rather level even though the terrain on both sides is quite
rugged. This is due entirely to the presence of a notch cut through the hills
like a dagger-wound. Whether this is a natural phenomena or the remnant of some
ancient construction project cannot be determined, but travelers certainly
benefit from its existence.
name: Old East Road

desc:
A very steep ridge running from southwest to northeast meets the road here, but
the road passes through a narrow cleft and manages to stay almost level. If this
cleft is artificial, it was excavated centuries ago because the walls are well
eroded.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road winds through an area of hills to the south and a plain sloping upwards
to the north. East and west of here the road continues. To the north-east, a
remarkably tall and steep hill can be seen.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road passes through a region of small hillocks, which grow larger as one
moves to the east. The surrounding countryside supports a few sparse patches of
forest, but for the most part the land is grassy. To the east, some of the
larger hills begin to support some forested areas.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Flat plains, with only a few shrubberies and even fewer flowers slope down to
the south, where the Old East Road stretches from east to west. East of here
steep, dangerous-looking hills stab at the sky, and far to the north the edges
of a vast forest can be seen where the plains end. A small and somewhat
demolished mound, overgrown with thorny bushes and grass stands here.
name: Sloping Plains

desc:
The plains slope down from the north towards the Old East Road, south of here.
Heather plants grow beside the road, and boulders of different sizes are spread
out all over the slopes. Embedded in the grass are wooden splinters from what
must have been several wagons many years ago. Far north of here, the border of a
forest is visible.
name: Heathery Slope

desc:
On the south side of the road, a rich forest spreads out. East of here the road
digs deeper into the hills, but to the west the ground flattens some. To the
north, rugged hills make it impossible to leave the road.
name: Old East Road

desc:
To the north, a set of extremely rugged hills prevents one from leaving the
road, as the ground quickly deteriorated into eroded gullies choked with
undergrowth. South of the road the hills aren't nearly as severe, and the
pockets of forest are full of game.
name: Old East Road

desc:
To the north, many rugged hills present a formidable challenge to anyone
traveling off of the road. The hills are gentler to the south, but there is
quite a lot of vegetation. To the west, a very steep (but narrow) ridge cuts
across the road at an angle.
name: Old East Road

desc:
All earth on the eastern side of this hill has eroded away, leaving a clean and
sheer rockface. Beyond the rockface a range of smaller and not as rugged hills
cover the landscape. The Old East Road lies south of here, but to get there one
would have to go far west before it is possible to climb down from the tall
hills. To the north, more hills lie scattered over the land.
name: Tall, Steep Hill

desc:
On top of this rugged hill, a clump of trees provides a natural wind-shelter as
it surrounds a small dip in the hill-crown. To the south a majestic view of the
Old East Road opens up, but climbing down the steep and rubble-covered hillside
would be impossible. The hills slopes down to a number of smaller rocky hills to
the west, but east of here another hill stands as tall and steep. Some smaller,
smoother hills stand to the north.
name: Tall, Steep Hill

desc:
This area is covered with rocky hills and small hillocks between them. On the
ground boulders, rocks and rubble makes travel hard. Strong-looking young
spruces form small clumps on the tops of some of the hills. To the south the Old
East Road stretches from east to west, and west of here the hills slopes
downwards into a very narrow crevice-like canyon. East of here the hills grow
taller, and there is no way of seeing past them.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
The landscape here is composed of many small hillocks. Their rounded tops are
often completely free of vegetation, but in the shallow depressions between
there are often small stand of trees. The Old East Road lies north of here, and
some hills stand to the east. West of here a flat, grassy area resides.
name: Light Forest

desc:
The ground is a bit rougher here than to the west, and hills begin to dominate
the landscape. To the north, the ground becomes much gentler and glimpses of the
Old East Road are visible from the northernmost of the hills. The range of hills
continues to the northeast --- the road passes through a notch in the hills, so
a traveler would not have an arduous journey through the region, provided he
stays on the road.
name: Rugged Hills

desc:
Right to the north, the Old East Road runs through a notch in this range of
rugged hills. Travel is treacherous, as often steep slopes are hidden beneath
thick brush, and in the vales between the hills the trees grow close and
tangled.
name: Rugged Hills

desc:
Immediately to the west, a tall and razor-like ridge precludes any movement in
that direction. The ridge ends abruptly a short distance to the north; it almost
looks as if it has been sliced to allow passage for the road which lies a short
distance away. In general, the terrain here is hills, broken by occasional
patches of dense forest. The forests seem to dominate more in the northern
areas.
name: East of a Ridge

desc:
The hills here are fairly rugged. From the crests of the taller ones, the brown
ribbon of the Old East Road can be seen a few miles to the north. Some of the
lower hills are covered with pine and spruce, and in the shaded dells, tangled
masses of rhododendrons make movement a chore.
name: Forested Hills

desc:
The lower-lying areas in the region are generally havens for small patches of
forest, while the tops of the hills (some with slopes quite severe) stick out
above the trees like rocks from the sea. The terrain seems much the same in
every direction, but to the north the Old East Road is visible from vantage
points on top of the hills.
name: Forested Hills

desc:
To the east, the forest abruptly ends in a large clearing. The ground is fairly
hilly, and in the small dales between hillcrests the forest is quite thick.
Small game-trails crisscross the area, and the calling of ravens is heard quite
often. To the north, at the bottom of a slope, the Old East Road can be espied.
name: Forest among the Hills

desc:
The walls of this cave are jagged and cracked, as if somebody would have created
with open space in the hill with sheer force. The granite is dark grey and
spotted with dried blood-stains. The floor is rocky here, tiny streams of water
trickle between the small rocks to the lower eastern cave.
name: Trolls' Treasure Chamber

desc:
A large stone door is set into the eastern wall of this blackened cave. Marks of
charcoal and dried blood can be seen on the walls and floor. A round opening to
the north leads to a downwards sloping tunnel. A sharp, strong smell makes the
atmosphere thick in here. A stonedoor is set in the eastern wall, and high above
it a large, dark hole leads into the darkness.
name: Entrance to a Dreadful Cave

desc:
To the west a quite large rock blocks passage into the cave beyond, and an even
larger boulder is used as barricade to the south. Here the rock walls and
ceiling are dark red-brown, smooth and sooted. The earthen floor is too hard for
anything to leave tracks. To the east the cave continues.
name: Dreadful Cave
